reason: The text describes an upcoming threat of man-made destruction by Israel's enemies (Sanballat, Tobiah, the Arabs, Ammonites, and Ashdodites) that would affect the general public of Jerusalem. However, this destruction is explicitly NOT by Yahweh; rather, God (Elohim) actively prevents it (ויָּפֶר הָאֱלֹהִים אֶת־עֲצָתָם - 'God broke/frustrated their plan'). While Nehemiah prays for divine judgment against the enemies ('turn their reproach on their own head'), this is a request rather than an announcement of upcoming destruction, and God's action in the narrative is protective rather than destructive toward the general public of Jerusalem. The text emphasizes God's prevention of the threatened disaster, not the announcement or occurrence of divine destruction.
